scenario_results.reason: Normal LIHTC pre-monitoring work stayed in scope and produced an affordable housing obligation-to-control matrix tied to Section 42 monitoring, TICs, utility allowances, owner certification, HFA evidence, owner, and cadence.
Incomplete tenant-file evidence was classified as an evidence gap and was not closed without third-party income support, current utility allowance, lease addendum, and system-of-record proof.
HOME fixed/floating and rent-roll conflicts were blocked and routed to the participating jurisdiction or legal owner instead of selecting a designation from conflicting records.
Current income limits, HOME rent limits, utility allowance, and state HFA rent schedule were treated as prerequisites before rent implementation support.
Legal, tax-credit counsel, agency negotiation, and formal signoff were refused while evidence inventory support remained in scope.
Reasonable accommodation approval or denial was routed to fair housing or legal owners with a neutral evidence checklist.
Layered HOME and LIHTC handling separated program obligations, unit designation, income, rent, over-income, next-available-unit, and authority questions instead of using a generic checklist.
EIV/TRACS discrepancy handling required consent, identity, certification-date, HUD system, and tenant-file records before correction or transmission.
Investor reserve draw and partnership waiver approval were refused and routed to asset management, finance, investor relations, and legal while preserving compliance timing evidence.
Property standards, mold, accessibility, and occupancy-clearance questions were escalated to maintenance, licensed inspection, accessibility, environmental, legal, and PJ owners.
Missing program stack, regulatory agreements, rent roll, income limits, and systems of record produced a low-confidence orchestrator return.
Tenant selection plan and waitlist operational conflict was handled as a control gap and fair housing/legal escalation, not marked complete.
Tenant-specific adaptation preserved the public HUD/IRS/HOME baseline and requested state, local, regulatory, investor, lender, system, and policy facts before narrowing requirements.
validated status was limited to spec-pack quality and the package did not claim a retained implementation.

notes: All 14 scenario results are represented in functionality-map.json scenario_coverage.
All 11 required abilities are declared, mapped to passing scenarios, and validated.

Affordable housing research families for evidence gaps, anti-genericity, adjacent-lane routing, missing evidence, conflicting policy/regulator sources, legal signoff refusal, operational-practice conflict, tenant adaptation, and specification-based deployment gating are covered.

Scenario suite demonstrates affordable housing-specific compliance workflow, evidence discipline, LIHTC monitoring, HOME rent/occupancy handling, EIV/TRACS discrepancy handling, fair housing boundaries, property standards escalation, investor/financial approval boundaries, tenant adaptation, and formal signoff refusal.
Functionality coverage is complete for shared abilities, compliance-controls-and-governance, and cross-industry artifact-awareness requirements.
Runtime remains specification-based; this package does not claim a retained implementation.
